Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Understand the Goal The user wants a DuckDB SQL query that Takes an event_log table with case_id activity timestamp Constructs the ordered sequence of activities per case_id sorted by timestamp Groups cases by this sequence to find unique process variants Counts cases per variant finds the top K variants by frequency Returns all original events from cases that belong to these top K variants K should be a parameter or defined in the query

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Key Steps SQL Concepts Step 1 Order events per case and construct sequence Need STRING_AGG with ORDER BY timestamp This gives the variant string per case Step 2 Count cases per variant Group by the variant string count case_id Step 3 Find top K variants Order by count descending limit K Step 4 Filter original events Join back to the original event_log using case_id that matches the top K variants Step 5 Return events Select all columns from event_log for those cases

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Draft Step by Step Mental SQL WITH case_variants AS SELECT case_id STRING_AGG activity ORDER BY timestamp AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id variant_counts AS SELECT variant COUNT case_id AS case_count FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant top_k_variants AS SELECT variant FROM variant_counts ORDER BY case_count DESC LIMIT 5 top_k_cases AS SELECT case_id FROM case_variants WHERE variant IN SELECT variant FROM top_k_variants SELECT el FROM event_log el JOIN top_k_cases tkc ON el case_id equals tkc case_id ORDER BY el case_id el timestamp

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Refine and Check DuckDB Compatibility STRING_AGG with ORDER BY is valid in DuckDB CTEs are standard and well supported LIMIT K is fine Need to ensure the final output returns all events from the original log for those cases The JOIN does that Should I order the final result ordering by case_id timestamp is good practice

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: Another approach Use ARRAY_AGG or LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp which is often better in DuckDB for exact matching without delimiter collisions I will stick with STRING_AGG for readability

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: If ties matter RANK or DENSE_RANK could be used but LIMIT is standard for top K What if two activities have the exact same timestamp in a case The prompt does not specify tie breaking so ORDER BY timestamp is sufficient for the prompt

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Parameter binding DuckDB supports parameter styles but for a standalone query it is safer to use LIMIT 5 with a comment or a params CTE Better use WITH params K AS VALUES 5 to make it fully self contained and parameterizable

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Lets verify the logic case_variants groups by case_id and aggregates activities ordered by timestamp Correct variant_counts counts cases per variant Correct top_k_variants picks top K by count Correct relevant_cases gets case_ids for those variants Correct Final SELECT joins back to event_log Correct

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: WITH params AS SELECT 5 AS K case_variants AS SELECT case_id STRING_AGG activity ORDER BY timestamp AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id variant_counts AS SELECT variant COUNT case_id AS case_count FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant top_k_variants AS SELECT variant FROM variant_counts ORDER BY case_count DESC LIMIT SELECT K FROM params filtered_cases AS SELECT case_id FROM case_variants WHERE variant IN SELECT variant FROM top_k_variants SELECT el FROM event_log el JOIN filtered_cases fc ON el case_id equals fc case_id ORDER BY el case_id el timestamp

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Final check of the prompt Return all events from the original event log that belong only to cases which are part of these top K variants Matches Correctly extract the ordered sequence of activities per case Matches Aggregate and count cases per variant Matches Filter down to the cases belonging to the top K variants Matches Return the corresponding events from those filtered cases Matches All good
